weeks.title: Week 1 — Java fundamentals + dev tools setup
weeks.description: Get your environment ready, then write your first real Java programs.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Install everything

tasks.text: Install JDK 21 (LTS) from adoptium.net
tasks.description: The JDK is the compiler and runtime every Java and Spring Boot project needs — 21 is the current LTS version.

tasks.text: Install IntelliJ IDEA Community Edition
tasks.description: IntelliJ understands Java deeply — autocomplete, refactoring, and run/debug buttons save hours over a plain text editor.

tasks.text: Install MySQL Community Server + MySQL Workbench
tasks.description: MySQL is the database every phase of this curriculum builds on, and Workbench lets you inspect tables and run queries visually.

tasks.text: Install VS Code with extensions: ESLint, Prettier, Live Server
tasks.description: VS Code with these extensions is what you'll use for the JavaScript and React work starting in Phase 3.

tasks.text: Install Git and create a GitHub account
tasks.description: Git tracks every change you make, and GitHub is where every project in this curriculum will be hosted.

tasks.text: Create a GitHub repo called "phase1-practice" and clone it
tasks.description: Committing from day one builds the habit — by Phase 5 you'll depend on Git for branching and CI/CD.

days.resource: Resource: adoptium.net for JDK · jetbrains.com/idea for IntelliJ · dev.mysql.com/downloads for MySQL

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Java: variables, types, operators

tasks.text: Learn: int, double, String, boolean data types
tasks.description: These are the basic building blocks every Java program is made of — you'll declare variables of these types in every class you write.

tasks.text: Learn: arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %)
tasks.description: Arithmetic operators are how you'll calculate prices, totals, and averages throughout this curriculum.

tasks.text: Learn: String concatenation and System.out.println()
tasks.description: println() is your first debugging tool — printing values is how you'll check your code is doing what you expect.

tasks.text: Practice: write a program that calculates area of a rectangle
tasks.description: A small program combining variables, a formula, and output — your first complete Java program.

tasks.text: Practice: write a simple temperature converter (C to F)
tasks.description: Practicing a formula and conversion reinforces variables and arithmetic operators together.

days.resource: Resource: W3Schools Java tutorial (free) · Bro Code Java on YouTube — Episode 1–5

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Java: conditionals and loops

tasks.text: Learn: if / else if / else statements
tasks.description: Branching logic like this is how your code makes decisions — used in almost every method you'll write from here on.

tasks.text: Learn: switch statements
tasks.description: switch is a cleaner alternative to long if/else chains when checking a fixed set of values.

tasks.text: Learn: for loop and while loop
tasks.description: Loops let you repeat work — looping over students, products, or database rows is something you'll do constantly.

tasks.text: Practice: FizzBuzz (print 1–100, but Fizz for multiples of 3, Buzz for 5)
tasks.description: FizzBuzz is a classic exercise that combines loops and conditionals — a good gut check before moving on.

tasks.text: Practice: Simple number guessing game using Scanner for user input
tasks.description: Using Scanner for input is your first interactive program — the same pattern your Phase 1 CLI project will use.

days.resource: Resource: Bro Code Java — Episode 6–12 · Try exercises on exercism.org (Java track)

tasks.text: Learn: defining and calling methods with parameters and return types
tasks.description: Methods let you organize code into reusable, named pieces — the foundation of every Java class you'll build.

tasks.text: Learn: method overloading
tasks.description: Overloading shows how Java picks the right method based on the arguments passed — useful for flexible APIs.

tasks.text: Learn: arrays — declaring, initializing, iterating
tasks.description: Arrays are the simplest way to store a collection of values — a stepping stone to ArrayList and database query results.

tasks.text: Practice: write a method that finds the max value in an array
tasks.description: Combining a loop with comparison logic inside a method is a pattern you'll reuse constantly in service code.

tasks.text: Practice: write a method that reverses a String
tasks.description: String manipulation practice — useful for formatting output and validating input later on.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Git workflow + week review

tasks.text: Learn Git: git add, git commit -m, git push, git pull
tasks.description: These four commands are your daily Git routine — you'll type them dozens of times across this curriculum.

tasks.text: Learn Git: git status, git log, creating branches
tasks.description: Branches let you work on a feature without breaking your main code — essential from Phase 4 onward.

tasks.text: Push all this week's practice files to GitHub
tasks.description: Pushing your work means it's backed up and visible — start building your GitHub history now.

tasks.text: Review: re-read any code you wrote that felt confusing
tasks.description: Revisiting confusing code while it's still fresh helps it stick — don't skip this step.

tasks.text: Challenge: build a simple BMI calculator using methods
tasks.description: A self-contained challenge that combines variables, arithmetic, and methods from this week into one small program.

days.resource: Resource: learngitbranching.js.org — interactive Git tutorial (free, browser-based)

weeks.askPrompt: Can you give me a Java exercise for a complete beginner — something practical that uses variables, conditionals, loops, and methods together?

weeks.title: Week 2 — Java OOP + MySQL basics
weeks.description: Learn object-oriented programming, the heart of Spring Boot, then start SQL.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Java: classes and objects

tasks.text: Learn: what a class is vs what an object is
tasks.description: Classes are blueprints and objects are instances of them — this distinction underlies every entity you'll build in Spring Boot.

tasks.text: Learn: fields (instance variables), constructors
tasks.description: Fields store an object's data, and constructors set that data up when the object is first created.

tasks.text: Learn: getters and setters (encapsulation)
tasks.description: Getters and setters control access to fields — a pattern Spring Boot relies on for entities and DTOs.

tasks.text: Practice: create a Student class with name, age, grade fields
tasks.description: A first hands-on class with multiple fields — practice modeling a real-world thing in code.

tasks.text: Practice: create multiple Student objects and print their data
tasks.description: Creating multiple objects from one class shows how the same blueprint produces independent instances.

days.resource: Why it matters: In Spring Boot, every entity (Product, User, Order) is a Java class. This is the foundation.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Java: inheritance and interfaces

tasks.text: Learn: extends keyword — child class inherits parent
tasks.description: Inheritance lets one class reuse and extend another's behavior — reduces duplication across related classes.

tasks.text: Learn: @Override and polymorphism
tasks.description: @Override and polymorphism let different subclasses respond differently to the same method call.

tasks.text: Learn: interface — defining a contract a class must follow
tasks.description: An interface defines what a class must do without saying how — the basis for Spring's repository pattern.

tasks.text: Practice: create Animal → Dog → Cat hierarchy with a speak() method
tasks.description: A classic hierarchy exercise that makes inheritance and polymorphism concrete and easy to visualize.

tasks.text: Practice: create a Printable interface, implement it in two classes
tasks.description: Implementing the same interface in two classes shows how a shared contract enables flexible, swappable code.

days.resource: Why it matters: Spring Boot uses interfaces extensively — JpaRepository is an interface. You extend it, Spring implements it for you.

tasks.text: Learn: ArrayList — add, remove, get, iterate with for-each
tasks.description: ArrayList is a resizable list — you'll use it constantly to hold collections of objects like students or products.

tasks.text: Learn: HashMap — put, get, containsKey, iterate entrySet
tasks.description: HashMap gives you fast lookups by key — useful for things like looking up a product by ID.

tasks.text: Learn: try-catch-finally for handling exceptions
tasks.description: Exception handling lets your program recover from errors instead of crashing — critical for any real application.

tasks.text: Practice: student registry using ArrayList of Student objects
tasks.description: Combining a custom class with a collection mirrors the shape of most service-layer code in Spring Boot.

tasks.text: Practice: phone book using HashMap (name → phone number)
tasks.description: A simple key-value lookup exercise that mirrors how maps and caches are used later in the curriculum.

tasks.text: Practice: add validation to your Student class — throw new IllegalArgumentException("message") if age < 0, catch it in main() and print the message without crashing
tasks.description: Throwing and catching a custom validation exception is exactly the pattern Spring Boot uses to reject bad input.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: MySQL: first queries

tasks.text: Open MySQL Workbench, connect to local MySQL server
tasks.description: Connecting to your database is the first step before you can store or retrieve any data.

tasks.text: Learn: CREATE DATABASE, USE, CREATE TABLE with column types
tasks.description: These commands create the database and table structures that will hold your application's data.

tasks.text: Learn: INSERT INTO, SELECT * FROM, WHERE clause
tasks.description: INSERT and SELECT with WHERE are the two most common operations you'll perform on any table.

tasks.text: Learn: UPDATE and DELETE with WHERE (never without!)
tasks.description: UPDATE and DELETE change existing data — always test the WHERE clause with a SELECT first to avoid mistakes.

tasks.text: Practice: create a students table, insert 5 rows, query them
tasks.description: A hands-on exercise that exercises CREATE, INSERT, and SELECT together on real data.

days.resource: Resource: sqlbolt.com — interactive SQL lessons in browser, completely free

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: MySQL: JOINs and relationships

tasks.text: Learn: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY — what they do and why
tasks.description: Primary and foreign keys are how relational databases link related rows together — the foundation of schema design.

tasks.text: Learn: INNER JOIN — combine rows from two related tables
tasks.description: INNER JOIN combines matching rows from two tables — essential for queries that span related entities.

tasks.text: Learn: LEFT JOIN — include rows even if no match on right
tasks.description: LEFT JOIN includes unmatched rows too — useful when related data might not exist yet.

tasks.text: Learn: ORDER BY, LIMIT, GROUP BY, COUNT()
tasks.description: These clauses sort, limit, and summarize results — used in almost every reporting query you'll write.

tasks.text: Practice: create a courses table, link to students via enrollment table, write JOIN query
tasks.description: Modeling a many-to-many relationship with an enrollment table mirrors real schema design from Phase 2 onward.

days.resource: Resource: Complete sqlbolt.com lessons 1–12 · Try sqlfiddle.com to experiment

weeks.askPrompt: Explain Java interfaces to me like I have no programming experience — with a simple real-world analogy and a short code example

weeks.title: Week 3 — JavaScript fundamentals
weeks.description: Learn the language of the browser — you'll need this for React in Phase 3.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: JS: variables, functions, scope

tasks.text: Learn: var vs let vs const — always use const/let
tasks.description: let and const avoid the bugs that var's function-scoping can cause — the modern standard for declaring variables.

tasks.text: Learn: function declarations vs arrow functions (=>)
tasks.description: Arrow functions are everywhere in React — getting comfortable with the syntax now pays off later.

tasks.text: Learn: scope — what variables are accessible where
tasks.description: Understanding scope prevents confusing bugs where a variable isn't accessible where you expect it to be.

tasks.text: Learn: template literals — `Hello ${name}!`
tasks.description: Template literals make building strings with variables much cleaner than concatenation with +.

tasks.text: Practice: rewrite your Java temperature converter in JavaScript
tasks.description: Reimplementing a familiar program in JS highlights the syntax differences from Java while reusing logic you already know.

days.resource: Resource: javascript.info — the best free JS reference. Start at "JavaScript Fundamentals".

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: JS: arrays and objects

tasks.text: Learn: array methods — map(), filter(), find(), forEach()
tasks.description: map(), filter(), and find() are how you'll transform and search data fetched from your API in React.

tasks.text: Learn: objects — key-value pairs, dot notation, destructuring
tasks.description: Objects and destructuring are how API responses are shaped and consumed throughout React.

tasks.text: Learn: spread operator (...) for arrays and objects
tasks.description: The spread operator is the standard way to copy and update arrays/objects immutably — critical for React state updates.

tasks.text: Practice: array of product objects, filter by price, map to display names
tasks.description: Filtering and mapping over objects mirrors exactly how you'll render a product list from an API response.

tasks.text: Practice: use destructuring to extract fields from an object
tasks.description: Destructuring makes pulling fields out of API responses concise and readable.

days.resource: Why it matters: In React, you will use map() to render lists and filter() constantly. This is not optional knowledge.

tasks.text: Learn: document.getElementById(), querySelector()
tasks.description: Selecting elements is how vanilla JS finds things to update — React replaces this, but understanding it helps you appreciate why.

tasks.text: Learn: changing text with .textContent and HTML with .innerHTML
tasks.description: Updating text and HTML directly shows what React's virtual DOM abstracts away for you.

tasks.text: Learn: addEventListener() for click and input events
tasks.description: Event listeners are how the browser responds to user actions — React wraps this in handlers like onClick.

tasks.text: Practice: build a simple to-do list — add items, mark done, delete
tasks.description: A to-do list touches state, rendering, and events all together — a precursor to thinking in React components.

tasks.text: Practice: build a live character counter for a text input
tasks.description: A live counter is a small reactive UI — good practice before React makes this kind of update automatic.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: JS: Promises and async/await

tasks.text: Learn: what a Promise is — pending, fulfilled, rejected
tasks.description: Promises represent a value that isn't ready yet — the foundation of every API call you'll make in JS.

tasks.text: Learn: .then().catch() chaining
tasks.description: Chaining .then()/.catch() is the original way to handle async results and errors.

tasks.text: Learn: async function and await keyword
tasks.description: async/await is the modern, readable syntax for working with Promises — used throughout TanStack Query in Phase 3.

tasks.text: Learn: try-catch inside async functions
tasks.description: try-catch around await is how you'll handle failed API calls in React.

tasks.text: Practice: fetch data from a public API (jsonplaceholder.typicode.com) and display it
tasks.description: Fetching real data from an API is the exact pattern you'll use to talk to your Spring Boot backend later.

days.resource: Resource: javascript.info — "Promises, async/await" chapter. This is the most important JS concept for API work.

tasks.text: Learn: ES modules — export and import between files
tasks.description: ES modules let you split code across files — exactly how every React project is organized.

tasks.text: Learn: npm basics — what it is, npm init, installing packages
tasks.description: npm is how every JS/React dependency gets installed — you'll use it constantly from Phase 3 onward.

tasks.text: Practice: refactor to-do list into multiple files using modules
tasks.description: Splitting a working program into modules practices the file organization you'll use in React.

tasks.text: Push all JS work to GitHub
tasks.description: Pushing your JS work keeps your portfolio growing and reinforces the Git habit from Week 1.

tasks.text: Review: what felt confusing this week? Write down 3 questions.
tasks.description: Naming what felt confusing turns vague unease into specific things you can revisit and resolve.

weeks.askPrompt: Explain JavaScript Promises and async/await with a simple real-world analogy — no jargon, beginner-friendly

weeks.title: Week 4 — Advanced Java + SQL + consolidation
weeks.description: Fill gaps, deepen understanding, and prepare for the Phase 1 project.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Java: generics and Optional

tasks.text: Learn: what generics are — List<String> vs raw List
tasks.description: Generics let collections be type-safe — List<String> won't let you accidentally add something that isn't a String.

tasks.text: Learn: Optional<T> — avoid NullPointerException
tasks.description: Optional forces you to explicitly handle the 'no value' case — Spring Data repositories return it constantly.

tasks.text: Learn: Java Streams — filter(), map(), collect() on collections
tasks.description: Streams let you transform collections in a declarative pipeline — a common pattern in Spring Boot service methods.

tasks.text: Practice: rewrite your student registry using Stream operations
tasks.description: Rewriting earlier code with Streams shows how the same logic can be expressed more concisely.

days.resource: Why it matters: Spring Boot's JpaRepository returns Optional — you'll see this every day.

tasks.text: Learn: enum type — fixed set of named constants
tasks.description: Enums model a fixed set of valid values — perfect for things like order status or pass/fail grades.

tasks.text: Learn: what annotations are (e.g. @Override) — metadata on code
tasks.description: Annotations are metadata that frameworks like Spring read to configure behavior — you'll see dozens of them.

tasks.text: Learn: static keyword and when to use it
tasks.description: static members belong to the class itself, not an instance — useful for constants and utility methods.

tasks.text: Practice: create an OrderStatus enum (PENDING, PROCESSING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED)
tasks.description: A status enum like this is the exact pattern used for order and request state machines in Spring Boot apps.

tasks.text: Practice: create a GradeStatus enum (PASS for score >= 50, FAIL for score < 50) for use in your Phase 1 project
tasks.description: Building this enum now sets up the PASS/FAIL logic your Phase 1 project's grade report needs.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: MySQL: advanced queries

tasks.text: Learn: aggregate functions — COUNT(), SUM(), AVG(), MAX(), MIN()
tasks.description: Aggregate functions summarize data across rows — essential for reports like averages and totals.

tasks.text: Learn: GROUP BY with HAVING clause
tasks.description: GROUP BY with HAVING filters aggregated groups — e.g. students whose average is below 50.

tasks.text: Learn: subqueries (SELECT inside SELECT)
tasks.description: Subqueries let one query feed into another — useful for 'find the max, then find rows matching it' logic.

tasks.text: Learn: indexes — why they speed up queries, how to create one
tasks.description: Indexes are how databases stay fast as tables grow — you'll revisit this in depth during Phase 5's performance work.

tasks.text: Practice: write 10 queries on your students/courses schema from Week 2
tasks.description: Writing a batch of queries against your own schema builds fluency faster than reading examples.

days.resource: Resource: Complete sqlbolt.com lessons 13–18 (aggregation and subqueries)

tasks.text: Add MySQL JDBC driver dependency to a Maven project
tasks.description: The JDBC driver is the library that lets Java talk to MySQL — required for any database access from Java.

tasks.text: Learn: DriverManager.getConnection() with URL, user, password
tasks.description: DriverManager.getConnection() is the starting point of every JDBC program — it opens the connection to your database.

tasks.text: Learn: PreparedStatement for INSERT and SELECT (avoid SQL injection)
tasks.description: PreparedStatement safely inserts user input into SQL — string concatenation is vulnerable to SQL injection.

tasks.text: Learn: ResultSet — loop through query results in Java
tasks.description: ResultSet is how you read rows back from a query — you'll loop through it to build Java objects from database rows.

tasks.text: Practice: Java program that reads students from MySQL and prints them
tasks.description: This program ties the whole chain together — connect, query, read results, print — the core of the Phase 1 project.

tasks.text: Design your Phase 1 project schema on paper (tables, columns, relationships)
tasks.description: Designing the schema on paper first avoids costly restructuring once you start writing code.

tasks.text: Create the MySQL database and tables for the project
tasks.description: Creating the real tables turns your design into something your Java code can connect to and query.

tasks.text: Plan the Java classes you'll need (Student, Course, etc.)
tasks.description: Planning your classes before coding helps you see how Student, Course, and Grade relate to each other.

tasks.text: Review your biggest weak spot from Weeks 1–3 and re-practice it
tasks.description: Revisiting your weakest topic now means you start the project on solid footing instead of stumbling mid-build.

tasks.text: Self-assessment: without looking at notes, write a Java class with inheritance and an interface, a SQL query with a JOIN and GROUP BY, and a JS async function that fetches data and handles errors
tasks.description: This self-assessment combines Java, SQL, and JS — exactly the cross-stack thinking Phase 2 builds on.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to connect Java to MySQL using JDBC with a simple working code example — PreparedStatement for INSERT and SELECT

weeks.title: Phase 1 project — student grade tracker
weeks.description: A real CLI app that uses everything you learned. Takes about 3–5 days to build.

projectCards.heading: What you'll build
projectCards.text: A Java command-line application where a user can add students, record their grades for subjects, and view a grade report — all stored in MySQL using JDBC.

tasks.text: Add a new student (name, age, email)
tasks.description: The first CRUD operation — every other feature builds on being able to create a student record.

tasks.text: Add a subject (Math, Science, English)
tasks.description: Subjects are the other side of the grade relationship — needed before you can record any grades.

tasks.text: Record a grade for a student in a subject (0–100)
tasks.description: This is the core write operation of the app — connects students, subjects, and scores together.

tasks.text: View all students
tasks.description: A basic SELECT * query — your first read feature and a quick way to verify data was saved correctly.

tasks.text: View one student's grades with average calculated and PASS/FAIL status per subject (using a GradeStatus enum)
tasks.description: Combines a JOIN, an aggregate (AVG), and your GradeStatus enum — the most complex feature in the project.

tasks.text: Find highest and lowest scorer per subject using SQL
tasks.description: Practices MAX/MIN with GROUP BY — a real reporting query like you'd write for an admin dashboard.

tasks.text: Handle invalid input gracefully (try-catch)
tasks.description: Wrapping input parsing in try-catch keeps the CLI from crashing on bad input — a basic but important UX detail.

tasks.text: Push finished project to GitHub with a README explaining it
tasks.description: A README turns this from 'code on your computer' into a portfolio piece a reviewer can understand.

projectCards.heading: Suggested class structure
projectCards.text: Student.java · Subject.java · Grade.java · GradeStatus.java (enum) · DatabaseConnection.java · StudentRepository.java · GradeRepository.java · Main.java

weeks.tip: When this project works end-to-end, you're genuinely ready for Phase 2. A working Java + MySQL project is also something you can show in your GitHub portfolio.
weeks.askPrompt: Help me build the Phase 1 project — a student grade tracker CLI app in Java with MySQL. Start with the database schema and the DatabaseConnection class.
